topics.title: Compliance Risk Profile (Solution Profile)
topics.description:
Description:
A Compliance Risk Profile, also known as a Solution Profile, is a structured assessment used to identify the compliance risks associated with a solution and determine the applicable IT compliance requirements. The profile helps ensure that solutions comply with legal, regulatory, corporate policy, and Pfizer Digital requirements throughout their lifecycle.
topics.content:
The Compliance Risk Profile process begins by creating a Solution Profile for a solution that is developed, supported, or managed by Pfizer Digital. Before profiling can start, the required deployment records must exist in the CMDB, and supplier/service records must be available if suppliers are responsible for any controls.
The Solution Profile Author creates the profile, associates the relevant solution and supplier records, and completes the profiling questionnaire. The questionnaire captures information about the solution's functionality, technology category, data usage, and compliance obligations. Based on the responses, the system determines the applicable compliance risk domains and generates the corresponding Compliance Requirements from the Common Requirements Set (CRS).
Once profiling is completed, the Solution Profile must be reviewed and confirmed by the appropriate stakeholders. Confirmation is required from the Solution Owner Representative and Digital Quality Assurance (BTQA). Additional reviewers such as Business Unit Quality Assurance (BUQA), Internal Controls Governance Council (ICGC), Privacy, Legal, or Security representatives may be required depending on the identified compliance domains.
After confirmation, the assigned Compliance Requirements must be evaluated to determine whether appropriate controls have been implemented. For each requirement, control information must be documented, including ownership, implementation status, control type, and control description. Requirements may be marked as Met, Met by Pfizer BT Service, Not Applicable, or Not Met. Any unmet requirement must either have a documented remediation plan or a formally approved risk acceptance.
The Solution Profile must be maintained throughout the solution lifecycle. Updates are required whenever significant changes occur, such as changes to functionality, data usage, compliance requirements, or remediation status. Even if no changes occur, the profile must be reviewed at least once every three years to ensure it remains accurate.
When a solution is retired or the profile is no longer required, the Solution Profile can be archived. Archived profiles remain available for record retention purposes but cannot be modified.
The completed Compliance Risk Profile provides a documented view of applicable compliance risks, assigned requirements, implemented controls, and stakeholder approvals, helping ensure ongoing compliance and effective risk management.

topics.title: Change Control Procedure (PGS Digital Solutions)
topics.description: The Change Control Procedure is a structured process for managing changes to computer systems, applications, and associated tools within PGS Digital Solutions. It ensures that all changes are evaluated, authorized, implemented, and documented with the appropriate level of control, maintaining compliance with quality, regulatory, and corporate standards.
topics.content:
The Change Control Procedure covers the full lifecycle of system changes, including initiation, assessment, implementation, and post-approval. The process ensures changes are controlled and risks are minimized.
1.	Initiation of a Change Request:
•	Any individual (Change Requestor) can identify the need for a change, including system users, business owners, or project team members.
•	The Change Originator logs the request into the Quality Tracking System (QTS), providing details such as the title, CI IDs, responsible person, change origin, validation/verification methodology, urgency, description, reason, and recommended actions.
•	The Responsible Person coordinates the change, ensuring all required information is captured, including criticality, scope, impact, and classification.
2.	Impact Assessment:
•	Project Team SMEs and PGS Digital Risk Management evaluate the change’s impact on system functionality, verification deliverables, testing, security (Minimum Security Baseline), data integrity, and regulatory compliance.
•	The assessment is documented in the QTS system, with supporting evidence attached as required.
3.	Action Identification:
•	Specific actions required to implement the change are recorded as Action Items, either as child items under the parent Change Request or separately.
•	Pre-production verification for GMP solutions, MSB scans, and technical or functional tasks are assigned to responsible SMEs.
•	Each action item includes a due date, evidence requirements, and recommended actions to ensure full implementation.
4.	Pre-Approvals:
•	Before implementation, the change request is reviewed by responsible parties based on its criticality. Approvers may include the Project Team, PGS Digital Risk Management, and Quality Authority.
•	Pre-approval confirms that impact assessments are complete, risks are understood, verification aligns with the change, and back-out plans are defined.
5.	Implementation of Change Post Approval:
•	The Responsible Person oversees the execution of all action items, ensuring changes are implemented according to approved plans.
•	Evidence of completion, including screenshots, documents, or test results, is attached and validated in the QTS system.
6.	Post-Approvals:
•	After all actions are completed, approvers review the change summary, supporting evidence, and compliance with the original assessment.
•	Approval finalizes the change, moving the record to a closed status. If additional work is required, the record is returned for correction.
7.	Emergency Changes:
•	Emergency changes are performed to address immediate threats to safety, product quality, or operations.
•	Verbal pre-approvals are obtained prior to execution, with documentation entered into the QTS system within two business days.
8.	Business Continuity:
•	If the QTS system is unavailable, changes are logged using paper-based forms, with cross-references and documentation maintained for later entry into QTS.
•	The Change Management Coordinator ensures proper numbering, logging, and filing of paper-based changes until electronic entry is possible.
The procedure ensures that every change is documented, assessed, authorized, implemented, and closed in a controlled manner. Proper segregation of duties is maintained to avoid conflicts of interest, and all associated records are stored for compliance and audit purposes.

topics.title: ADLC Enablement Procedure (JIRA, qTest, TOSCA)
topics.description: The ADLC Enablement Procedure defines the process for managing user stories, test cases, and test executions in a controlled environment using JIRA, qTest, TOSCA, and VERA. It ensures traceability, e-signature approvals, and compliance with GxP, SOX, and other controlled projects during the Agile Development Life Cycle (ADLC).
topics.content:
The ADLC Enablement Procedure covers activities from requirement capture to formal test execution and approval:
1.	User Story Management (JIRA/VERA):
•	User Stories capture business and functional requirements. Each story must include summary, acceptance criteria, risk rating, requirement criticality, compliance domain, and traceability to technical documentation.
•	User Stories are created, revised, and approved in JIRA. The VERA tool provides electronic signature approvals and maintains an audit trail.
•	Revisions are allowed for incomplete or missing information; approved stories deployed to Production cannot be changed in scope, and new stories are created for enhancements.
2.	Test Case Management (qTest/TOSCA/VERA):
•	Test cases are created in qTest, linked to approved User Stories, and include detailed steps, expected results, priority, and test type (e.g., GxP, SOX, Non-GxP).
•	TOSCA test cases can be created and linked to qTest to execute automated or manual tests. Pre-approval of test cases occurs via VERA.
•	Revisions to test cases require entering the reason for change and routing for approval. Versioning ensures traceability of updates.
3.	Test Execution (qTest/TOSCA):
•	Test runs are created from approved test cases and executed step-by-step in qTest or TOSCA, with actual results, dispositions, and attachments recorded as evidence.
•	Each test step must pass, fail, or be marked as not applicable (N/A), and defects are raised for deviations.
•	Test runs are routed for post-execution approval in VERA. Approvers verify completeness, accuracy, evidence attachment, and link defects if necessary. Rejected runs can be updated or re-executed.
4.	Release and Build Management:
•	Releases are created in JIRA and synchronized with qTest as the Target Release/Build. Builds can be optionally created in qTest.
•	Proper folder and module structure is maintained to organize user stories and test cases for traceability.
5.	Roles and Responsibilities:
•	Core Agile Team: Create and maintain user stories with acceptance criteria and risk ratings.
•	Agile Team (User Story Approval): Review and approve user stories and associated risk assessments.
•	qTest/TOSCA Project Admin & Agile Tester: Manage project settings, create test cases, modules, suites, cycles, and schedule executions.
•	Testers: Execute test cases, record results, raise defects, and route executed tests for approval.
•	Test Design & Execution Approvers: Verify pre- and post-execution test quality and compliance evidence.
•	BTQA/Digital Risk Management: Ensure alignment with regulatory and Pfizer procedures.
6.	Reporting:
•	VERA Print View provides record detail reports for approved or rejected user stories, test cases, defects, and executed test runs. Watermarks indicate Draft or Rejected status.
The ADLC Enablement procedure ensures consistent, controlled management of requirements and testing, provides traceability from user stories to test execution, and maintains regulatory compliance across Pfizer Digital projects.

topics.title: Requirements/User Story Management
topics.description: Defines how to create, approve, and revise user requirements in JIRA with proper compliance and traceability.
topics.content:
User Stories represent business and functional requirements and are created in JIRA using predefined templates. Each story must include key fields such as summary (user requirement), acceptance criteria (functional requirements), risk rating, classification, and compliance domain. Acceptance criteria must be clear, testable, and traceable. Stories are linked to releases using Fix Versions and integrated with qTest for testing.
Before approval, a risk assessment must be completed as per SDLC guidelines. Once ready, the story is routed for approval via VERA, where authorized roles (Business, Technical, BTQA) review and electronically sign. After approval, the story is locked and used as the basis for test case creation.
If updates are needed, the story must be withdrawn from approval, revised with proper comments, and resubmitted. Approved stories already deployed cannot be modified for scope; instead, a new story must be created and linked to testing.
